statement.markdown: Given an `m x n` matrix, if an element is 0, set its entire row and column to 0. Do this in-place. Follow the constraints that a row or column will only be zeroed if it contains at least one 0. The input matrix will be modified in-place, and you do not need to return anything. However, to verify the correctness of your solution, the modified matrix will be checked against the expected output.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains two space-separated integers `m` and `n`, representing the number of rows and columns in the matrix, respectively. The next `m` lines each contain `n` space-separated integers representing the elements of the matrix.
statement.outputFormat: The output will be the modified matrix where rows and columns containing 0 have been zeroed. The output matrix will be represented as `m` lines, each containing `n` space-separated integers.
statement.notes: You are allowed to use extra space that does not scale with input size, i.e., space that does not grow with the size of the input matrix, such as a constant amount of space to store the first row and first column status.

constraints.description: Number of rows in the matrix
Number of columns in the matrix
Values of the elements in the matrix

examples.explanation: The element at position (1,1) is 0, so the first and second row and column are zeroed.
Both rows and columns contain a 0, so the entire matrix is zeroed.

hints.text: To solve this problem, we first need to identify which rows and columns need to be zeroed. We can do this by iterating through the matrix and keeping track of the rows and columns that contain at least one zero.

We can use two separate data structures, such as arrays or sets, to keep track of the rows and columns that need to be zeroed. This will allow us to efficiently mark the rows and columns that contain zeros without modifying the original matrix.

After identifying the rows and columns to be zeroed, we can make a second pass through the matrix to actually set the corresponding elements to zero. This approach ensures that we do not accidentally zero out rows or columns that do not contain any zeros.

editorial.title: Set Matrix Zeroes
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by first identifying the rows and columns that contain at least one zero. This can be done by iterating over the matrix and storing the indices of the rows and columns with zeros in separate sets. Then, we can iterate over the matrix again and set the elements to zero if their row or column index is present in the sets.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking every element in the matrix for each element to determine if it should be set to zero. However, this approach is inefficient and would result in a time complexity of O(m * n^2) or worse, making it impractical for large matrices.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use the first iteration to identify the rows and columns to be zeroed, and then use this information to update the matrix in the second iteration. This approach still has a time complexity of O(m * n) but reduces the number of operations required.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using two sets to store the indices of the rows and columns that need to be zeroed. This approach has a time complexity of O(m * n) and a space complexity of O(m + n), making it efficient for large matrices. The use of sets allows for fast lookup and insertion of indices, making the solution more efficient.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by showing that every element in the matrix is checked exactly once and that the sets of rows and columns to be zeroed are updated correctly. The solution also handles the case where a row or column contains multiple zeros correctly, as the sets automatically eliminate duplicates.
editorial.dryRun: To test the solution, we can create a sample matrix with some zeros and run the algorithm to verify that the resulting matrix has the correct rows and columns zeroed.
commonMistakes: Not using sets to store the indices of rows and columns to be zeroed, resulting in slower lookup and insertion times.
Not checking for duplicates when storing the indices of rows and columns to be zeroed.
Not iterating over the matrix correctly, resulting in some elements being missed or processed multiple times.
Not handling the case where a row or column contains multiple zeros correctly.
Using a brute force approach that checks every element in the matrix for each element, resulting in a high time complexity.
